Q1.label: Question 1
Q1.scenario:
XYZ Health is a health and care insurance company operating in Country B. Country B has a large mature health and care insurance market. XYZ Health has been selling individual income protection business via financial advisors for many years.
XYZ Health’s current approach for underwriting new income protection business is to collect detailed information about the applicant’s health, family history, occupation and lifestyle via a telephone interview with the applicant. This information is then assessed by a human underwriter, who may ask for more information about the applicant from their doctor and, where appropriate, may require the applicant to have a further medical examination. XYZ Health will then either accept the applicant on ordinary rates, adjust the premium paid by the applicant or decline the case.

(i).question: Assess the suitability of this underwriting approach for income protection business.
(i).synopsis: Underwriting Principles, Income Protection Risk, Risk Assessment

Solution: When underwriting IP insurance risk, the insurer will want to be able to assess the likelihood that a person will take prolonged time off work in future due to sickness. [1]
And in particular identify applicants who could have a substantially higher risk of being off work than assumed in the insurer's standard premium rates [½]

IP is a complex product and is potentially more complex than other types of heath and care product to underwrite as there could be many different reasons which could keep people off work, such as back pain and stress-related incapacity which are not relevant for other types of health and care product. [1]
XYZ therefore needs sufficient information from applicants for IP to determine a complete picture of the applicant’s health and lifestyle [1]
and to establish the likelihood of a claim being made to ascertain whether they are able to offer IP cover and what the premium should be [1]
The current underwriting approach gives the insurer a wide range of information that is directly relevant to assessing the policyholder’s likelihood of being unable to work [½]
and this will allow XYZ to get a full picture of the risk of the applicant, and will allow the insurer to decide whether to accept or decline an individual [½]
and sufficient information to classify the applicants into broadly homogeneous risk groups [½]
so that an appropriate price can be charged to the policyholder that reflects the risks taken by XYZ. [½]
and detailed upfront underwriting process reduces the scope for anti-selection. [½]
The current underwriting approach will also allow the insurer to take a nuanced approach to assessing applications [½]
and will allow the insurer to decline the fewest possible number of people. [½]
Accurate underwriting assessment of policyholder potentially allows for more accurate reserving for policies. [½]
Accurate upfront underwriting assessment will also make claim management processes simpler and easier. [½]
However, this is potentially a time-consuming process, requiring a lot of human intervention [½]
which is potentially expensive for the insurer [½]
and possibly prone to error. [½]
Overall, the current underwriting approach is suitable for IP business [½]

(ii).question: Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of this underwriting approach from the perspective of XYZ Health’s customers.

Solution: Advantages An initial telephone interview approach may be easier to complete for policyholders than requiring the individual to fill out a long complex form. [½]
This approach allows the insurer to accurately assess the risk of the applicant; therefore the policyholder should pay a fair premium given their personal circumstances [1]
and allows the insurer to offer cover that reflects the actual health of the applicant so the applicant should know from the outset what they can and cannot claim for [½]
potentially giving the policyholder greater confidence that if they make a claim XYZ will pay the claim. [½]
The insurer should be able to accept lives in good health very quickly [½]
making the process relatively straightforward and quick for customers in good health. [½]

As underwriting is done up front there should be fewer disputes for the policyholder if they subsequently claim on the policy which should make the claim process quicker for the policyholder if they subsequently make a claim [½]
The insurers risk assessment process should be transparent to the policyholder. [½]
and therefore the underwriting process should be seen as treating the customer fairly. [½]
Disadvantages The underwriting process could take many weeks for more complex cases and could potentially be a very long process for the applicant. [1]
At the end of the underwriting process the applicant could still be rejected for cover [½]
or they may have to pay much more for cover than standard lives. [½]
The applicant may need to disclose more personal information during the underwriting process than they would prefer to. [½]
The applicant may need to have further investigations which they may prefer not to have. [½]
The applicant may have difficulty providing the information requested by the insurer over the phone [½]
or they may have difficulty understanding what is being asked by the insurer. [½]
There is a risk that the underwriting decision by the insurer could be made on incomplete or incorrect information. [½]

(iii).question:
XYZ Health has been approached by a specialist underwriting company (AutoUW).
AutoUW has developed an automated underwriting system that uses machine learning and artificial intelligence algorithms to underwrite individual risks. AutoUW will use a range of different data sources in the underwriting process. AutoUW has said that all underwriting decisions are taken in seconds and its automated underwriting system does not require any human intervention once it is operational. AutoUW has stated that their system has been successfully implemented by health and care insurance companies in other countries.
AutoUW has stated that by introducing its automated underwriting system for income protection business, XYZ Health would significantly improve the speed and consistency of XYZ Health’s underwriting. The reduction in human processing would also reduce XYZ Health’s underwriting costs.
XYZ Health’s board has asked for further information about AutoUW’s automated underwriting system.
Outline the additional information that should be obtained about AutoUW’s automated underwriting system.
(iii).synopsis: Automated Underwriting, Market Research, Implementation Costs

Solution: AutoUW Underwriting - Use in other countries In what countries does AutoUW operate its automated underwriting system? [½]
What health and care insurance companies have implemented AutoUW’s system? [½]
What types of health and care insurance product has the underwriting system been used for? [½]
In particular has AutoUW’s underwriting system been used to underwrite income protection business? [½]
Can XYZ speak to other companies that have implemented AutoUw’s system to understand how they have implemented the system and any issues arising? [½]
What is the regulatory / legislative environment like in the countries that AutoUW has implemented its underwriting system in? [½]
AutoUW Underwriting - Implementation What is the process for implementing the automated system? [½]
What are the costs for implementation likely to be? [½]
What other additional implementation costs are there likely to be? [½]
E.g. staff training costs [½]
How does AutoUW charge? [½]
For example: is there a charge per case? [½]
is the charge premium based? [½]
a flat monetary charge? [½]
is there a license fee? [½]
Underwriting system - Data What data sources are going to be used by the underwriting system? [½]
What risk factors will the underwriting system use? [½]
How does AutoUW check that no risk factors used are prohibited in Country B? [½]
What checks are done on the data to ensure the data is correct, error free etc? [½]
Where is the data kept in terms of jurisdiction? [½]
What limitations are there on the transfer of data between jurisdictions? [½]
What data security controls does AutoUW have in place? [½]
How does AutoUW protect against cyber risk? [½]
How does AutoUW ensure that it complies with local data laws? [½]
Underwriting system - Operational How is the underwriting done - by AutoUW or by the insurer? [½]
Can AutoUW provide an explanation of how the algorithms will work in practice? [½]
Is it possible to see examples of how the system will work for a set of sample clients? [½]
How does AutoUW ensure consistency between underwriting individual cases? [½]
How will the risk classification work? [½]
Will applicants either be accepted or declined? [½]
Will the system apply ratings to lives where appropriate? [½]
If so, how will the rating system work? [½]
How does AutoUW ensure there is no bias or discrimination in its operation? [½]
How does AutoUW ensure that it complies with any relevant local laws - e.g. on discrimination. [½]
Are there any areas of the system that can specifically be tailored for XYZ’s products. [½]
What support does AutoUW provide to XYZ once the automated underwriting systems are operating? [½]
As the automated underwriting system is underwriting IP business how will it assess the likelihood that a person will take prolonged time off work in future due to sickness. [½]
How will the automated system take into account factors relevant to income protection claims which are not relevant for other types of health and care product but are relevant for IP business? [1]
E.g. back pain [½]
stress-related incapacity [½]
Do AutoUW change or update the system and, if so, how frequently is this done? [½]
Does Auto UW provide ongoing training and support in the operation of their system? If so, what support is provided? [½]
Does the underwriting system provide ongoing information for management to understand how the underwriting system is working? [½]
Can AutoUW provide sufficient information to enable the insurer to do a cost versus benefit analysis of implementing the new system? [½]
AutoUW Underwriting - Regulator Has AutoUW had any regulatory issues with respect to the operation of its underwriting system issues in the countries in which it operates? [½]
If so what were the issues? [½]
And how were these issues resolved? [½]

(iv).question:
The board of XYZ Health also want to understand the possible impact on XYZ Health’s business of implementing AutoUW’s underwriting system for income protection business.
Discuss the possible impact on XYZ Health’s business of implementing AutoUW’s underwriting system for income protection business.
(iv).synopsis: Artificial Intelligence, Human vs Machine, Risk Classification

Solution: Data The existing claims data that XYZ Health has built up will potentially not be representative of future claims experience. [1]
Risk Classification It is possible that underwriting automation could allow more accurate risk assessments than the current underwriting approach. [1]
Which could allow better classification of risks in the future. [½]
However human underwriters may be able to offer additional insights, expertise, and judgment in assessing risks that an AI-based programs may not be able to provide [1]
Human expertise will be lost in the future as there is not going to be any intervention from underwriters in the future. [½]
Moving to automated underwriting could potentially reduce the risk of human bias in underwriting decision making [½]
although the new automated system could introduce new bias. [½]
Business volumes / mix Business volumes/mix could change but this will depend on how the new underwriting system will operate. [½]

If only applicants in good health are accepted by the new underwriting system and applicants that need to be rated or have exclusions are not then this could lead to a significant change in business mix [1]
and potentially a fall in new business. [½]
Alternatively, if the new underwriting processes are seen as efficient and quick then financial advisors could be more willing to use XYZ, possibly increasing business volumes. [½]
Customers may be more willing to take out IP business with XYZ if the underwriting requirements from the new underwriting system are perceived as being less onerous. [½]
Claims Outgo The change in underwriting approach could lead to a significant change in claims experience in the future. [½]
Claims experience could improve if more business taken on is in respect of ‘standard’ lives. [½]
It may take a number of years to build up credible claims experience for pricing/reserving due to the changes in underwriting approach [1]
Expenses The new automated underwriting system could potentially lead to lower underwriting costs… [½]
as the insurer will potentially not need underwriters anymore. [½]
which could lead to lower per policy expenses. [½]
However, the level of reduction could depend on the level and form of any AutoUW charge. [½]
It may be that AutoUW could charge per case and this will need to be factored into the expense assumptions. [½]
XYZ may be reluctant to make all of the underwriters redundant immediately so the level of expense reduction will depend on how many underwriters XYZ decides to keep. [1]
There will be an upfront development cost of implementing the new underwriting system [½]
the cost of which may need to be spread over new business for a number of years. [½]
XYZ will need to consider how any development costs will be allocated to new business [½]
and the period over which any development costs will be spread. [½]
Overheads could be spread over a smaller number of policies if more applicants are rejected under the new underwriting system [½]
which could lead to an increase in per policy expenses. [½]

Profitability impact As outlined above there could be significant changes (both positive and negative) caused by the new underwriting systems to: Business mix Business volumes Experience Expense allocation (½ mark per example; max 1 mark) [1]
The changes could have a significant impact on product profitability for XYZ’s new income protection business. [1]
XYZ should model this to understand the impact. [½]
Reserves / Capital The increased uncertainty on XYZ’s business may mean that it should increase the margins for prudence in its reserves and capital calculation [½]
Which could lead to an increase in both reserves and capital. [½]
Reinsurer XYZ Health should discuss the implications of the new underwriting system with its reinsurer to understand the reinsurer's view about the proposed change in underwriting process. [½]
and whether it had any concerns [½]
or advice about how to implement an automated underwriting system [½]
Competition A new, faster underwriting system could offer a competitive advantage in the market [½]
Potentially financial advisors will prefer the new faster underwriting system, which could see XYZ receive more new business. [½]
If so, can the new underwriting system cope with the increased volumes of business? [½]
However, if many more cases are declined than was previously the case, this could make life difficult for financial advisors and applicants, which could make them less likely to use XYZ. [½]
It is possible that XYZ could be forced to implement an automated underwriting system if its current underwriting system is seen as out of date relative to its competitors [½]
Reputation Faster, more consistent underwriting times could improve XYZ Health’s reputation with customers and financial advisors. [½]
Although problems with the new system such as inconsistent or obviously incorrect underwriting decisions could lead to reputational damage. [½]
Similarly underwriting systems that were deemed unlawful (e.g. due to bias or discrimination) could lead to significant reputational damage. [½]

Some potential customers may not be happy if they cannot get income protection cover now under the new under system, where previously they would have been able to get cover but been rated or had exclusions. [½]
Dependence The company will be completely dependent on the new underwriting system. [½]
Need to consider the possible effects if AutoUW cease to trade or its underwriting system did not work as expected. [½]
If things go wrong and the insurer has got rid of its trained underwriting staff it may not have sufficiently trained staff available to take the underwriting back in house. [1]
Staff XYZ will need to train staff in the use of the new underwriting system. [½]
XYZ will need to consider the effects on staff, particularly underwriters. Can they be redeployed within the company or will they be made redundant [½]
Implementation of new system could lead to an increase in staff dissatisfaction. [½]
Impact on the customer Some customers may not be happy with no human interaction [½]
Other Will need to review marketing literature, terms and conditions etc and revise as [½]
required. If successful, there may be a demand to extend this type of underwriting to other product lines. [½]

(v).question:
–3 XYZ Health has had an initial discussion with its financial regulator about introducing an automated underwriting system. The financial regulator has expressed some initial concerns about the potential impact of the automated underwriting system on the consumer.
Suggest possible concerns the financial regulator may have with AutoUW’s underwriting system.
(v).synopsis: Ethics & Discrimination, Data Privacy, Bias in AI

Solution: The potential for unfair discrimination if a prohibited characteristic, such as gender, is used in the underwriting process. [1]
Even if a protected characteristic is not specifically used, there is a risk that an automated underwriting system may use data sources which could be a proxy for prohibited characteristics [1]
The results of the automated underwriting system could be biased. [½]
The potential for the insurer to unethically use the data it has accumulated [½]
For example, data could originally have been accumulated about an individual for a different purpose, not related to underwriting; [½]
the individual may not have given their permission for the data to be used in this way. [½]
Where an insurer uses non-standard data sources then an applicant may not have consented for their data to be used in this way. [½]
Increased risk of unfair discrimination against protected classes because some of the factors used may be inaccurate or reflect historical and systemic biases [½]
The lack of human intervention in the underwriting process could lead to unfair or inaccurate decisions not being identified sufficiently quickly [½]
which may be a particular issue for people with more complex or unusual applications. [½]
The lack of transparency in the underwriting system may make it difficult for insurers to identify and correct errors or biases in the system. [½]
A lack of transparency in the underwriting system may also make it more difficult to understand and explain the model’s actual workings and output. [½]
Automated underwriting may not accurately consider the nuanced factors impacting an individual’s risk profile for income protection. [½]
Data quality could be poor with data errors, incomplete data sets, or biased data which could lead to incorrect conclusions and unfair outcomes from underwriting automation. [½]
The insurer may not accurately understand how the automated underwriting system is working in practice. [½]
Poor quality underwriting could ultimately lead to adverse impacts on the insurers profitability and possibly even its solvency. [½]
Over-reliance on third party technology could increase overall risk for insurer (e.g. failure of AutoUW). [½]
The regulator could be worried if there are no back up underwriting processes in place. [½]

(vi).question: Set out the actions XYZ Health could take to address the concerns identified in part (v).
(vi).synopsis: Model Governance, Bias Monitoring, Human Oversight

Solution: Have checks in place to identify bias or unfair decisions once the underwriting [½]
system is up and running. Ensure human underwriters still oversee the underwriting process to reduce the risk of unfair discrimination. [½]
Could sample cases to monitor that no bias or errors are creeping into the underwriting cases. [½]
Compare the underwriting decisions made using the AutoUW process against those mad eby the underwriters for a sample of cases [½]
Ensure that human underwriters still have the skills and ability to override the automated underwriting decisions where issues with underwriting for a case have been identified. [½]
Ensure the underwriting system is thoroughly checked before implementation, with any issues identified and corrected. [½]
Ensure that the implementation of automated underwriting has suitable governance and controls in place. [½]
Ensure that there is appropriate ongoing monitoring and governance oversight of the new automated underwriting model. [½]
Require a written undertaking that AutoUW will follow the requirements of laws in Country B governing the use of data. [½]
Ensure XYZ’s underwriters can understand and rationalise the decisions being made by the automated underwriting system prior to launch. [½]
Ensure there are clear communication channels in place for policyholders or financial advisors to feedback if any decisions look unreasonable. [½]
Ensure there is regular dialogue between AutoUW and XYZ Health to resolve any issues identified with the new automated underwriting model. [½]
Ensure there is appropriate staff training in place before implementing the new underwriting system. [½]
Ensure policyholders complaints about the new underwriting system are addressed promptly and the system is corrected where appropriate. [½]
Could look to phase in the new system gradually, only moving to the next phase of implementation, when each phase is clearly working. [½]
Ensure there is appropriate back up and contingency processes in place if the new underwriting systems were to fail. [½]

Q2.label: Question 2
Q2.scenario:
Country A is a developing country with independent public and private health care systems.
The public health care system in Country A is funded through general taxation and services are provided to citizens of Country A for free. Health care services are provided via local clinics and large regional hospitals, which are both staffed by nurses and doctors. The quality of care provided by the public health care system is low and waiting times for care are long.
In addition, there are a range of private doctors and hospitals that provide a high standard of care but require either upfront payment from the patient for treatment or confirmation that private medical insurance will cover the cost of treatment prior to the treatment being provided.
The government of Country A has published a proposal for one social insurance scheme to replace the existing private and public health care systems. The administration of the scheme will be centrally managed by the government. The social insurance scheme will purchase care from health care providers registered with the fund. Private medical insurance would no longer cover any benefits provided for under the social insurance scheme.

(i).question:
Comment on the proposal from the perspective of:
(a) the government.
(b) the medical insurance industry.
(c) a medical practitioner.
(d) citizens of country A.
(i).synopsis: Universal Healthcare, Political Promises, Economic Productivity

Solution: (a) Government Could be part of political promises to extend quality healthcare to all [½]
which could lead to increased support from voters at the next election. [½]
The system may be viewed as fair as all citizens will have access to the same care. [½]
And it could help equalise any gaps in the market in terms of the quality of health care received by the rich and the poor. [½]
This can in turn have positive economic outcomes in terms of improving productivity, [½]
reducing preventable illnesses and deaths, [½]
and boosting public morale. [½]
The government may believe that the proposal will unlock additional funding for healthcare [½]
If current spending on private healthcare can be redirected to the social insurance fund. [½]
However, this could create strain on the general tax revenue, which may not be sustainable in the long run, and could escalate faster than expected. [½]
The government may believe that they will be able to create efficiencies [½]
and/or negotiate better rates with providers [½]
which could lead to lower cost for healthcare. [½]
The government will need to consider how the new scheme should be funded. [½]
The government will need to take policy decisions and introduce any necessary legislation, regulations etc to set up the new scheme. [½]
The government will need to set up a body to carry out the administration of the scheme. [½]
The government will need to consider what processes it puts in place to ensure the scheme is administered appropriately. [½]
The government will need to consider how long it will take to set up the new scheme and if any transitional arrangements will be required. [½]
(b) Medical insurance industry The proposal suggests that medical insurance will no longer be allowed in its current form since PMI cover that substitutes state provision will not be allowed. [½]
The insurance industry will be concerned about the viability of the industry in the future [½]
and length of the transition time to the new scheme. [½]
If this is very short then it won’t have enough time to adjust its business model. [½]
The insurance industry is likely to not be happy as they will have invested lots of money in their existing business. [½]
New arrangements could have massive impact on jobs in the medical insurance industry. [½]
Insurers will need to diversify and introduce new products that perhaps complement but do not substitute state provision. [½]
Insurers will need to assess if these new products will remain financially viable into the future [½]
Or may need to consider new alternative lines of business [½]
which will take time and money to develop. [½]
E.g. critical illness (max 1 mark for 2 examples of lines of business) [1]
Lower new business volumes could lead to falling product profitability [½]
for example, due to overheads spread over a declining number of policies. [½]
Existing products will need to be discontinued/decommissioned which may include costly system redesign, [½]
And claims costs may increase if policyholders rush to get private health treatment funded by PMI before the new state-run system goes live. [½]
Competition may increase in what may soon become a small, niche area of insurance. [½]
This will drive out a number of existing participants. [½]
The industry may want to lobby government to ensure that the market continues to operate, [½]
Or the medical insurance industry may be able to partner with the state in other ways, to remain relevant. [½]
For example, in administering the new health system as opposed to underwriting health claims risk. [½]
There may still be an affluent part of the market that prefers private cover, and it may be possible to sell this segment of the market products that offer protection which state cover does not [½]
for example, cover for seeking health treatment overseas. [½]
(c) Medical practitioners There may be an impact on employment, salaries, working practices and conditions etc. [½]
The impact of the proposal may depend on where the medical practitioner currently operates. [½]
Practitioners working in the public sector may welcome the move, as it may alleviate some of the burden and waiting times they have to deal with at public hospitals. [½]
Conversely, they may be concerned at higher standard of care that the new system may demand, since the public facilities already struggle to provide an acceptable level of care. [½]
Practitioners in the private sector may be concerned about payment timelines and scheme management. [½]
Although centralised payment may make practice administration simpler. [½]
Practitioners in the private system would be concerned at the additional volume of patients this proposal might create for them. [½]
Particularly since the public health system is poor, and citizens might flock to private facilities to receive care under the new system. [½]
This could lead to private facilities now being over-burdened and result in a drop in [½]

the standard of care being provided. This drop in standards can expose practitioners to the risk of litigation and claims of medical malpractice. They may require professional indemnity insurance to protect them in this regard. [½]
The process to register a practice with the state fund may be cumbersome and some practices may not receive state approval to register. [½]
New scheme may have defined medical protocols that could impact medical practitioners’ autonomy. [½]
(d) Citizen The impact of the proposal may depend on whether the individual currently has medical insurance (or the means to fund private care) or not. [½]
Citizens who cannot afford insurance premiums will be concerned about whether they will be covered by the new insurance scheme. [½]
Citizens who have relied on public healthcare might welcome the proposal, as it may be seen as a move towards greater equality in the country, [½]
And they are likely to welcome the increase in capacity which may improve waiting times [½]
and the quality of treatment and care received by the citizen. [½]
However, they may be concerned about the potentially greater tax burden this proposal will bring. [½]
Those who have utilised the private health sector, may be concerned about what this means about the standard of health care they can expect going forward. [½]
They may be concerned that qualified health professionals may choose not register with the state system; for example, they may potentially leave the country and operate elsewhere. [½]
They may be concerned about the potential tax burden of this system being shifted to them, which may prove more costly than the PMI premiums they are currently paying. [½]
Given the current state of public healthcare, citizens in general may be concerned about whether the state can be trusted to effectively provide care across the whole population. [½]
Citizens in general will want to know how the scheme is to be financed and what the financial impact will be on them. [½]
Citizens in general may also want to understand why they are being left with no insurance alternatives if they do not want to utilise the state proposed system. [½]
Citizens in general will want to know how much choice they have in the clinic and hospitals they can go to if requiring treatment. [½]
There could be a restricted choice of hospitals as only registered hospitals are covered by the scheme. [½]

(ii).question: Discuss how the proposed social insurance scheme could be financed.
(ii).synopsis: Healthcare Financing, Taxation Models, Government Debt

Solution: Financing of the scheme needs to consider Revenue collection for the scheme, [½]
Pooling and [½]
Purchasing of services [½]
The government could increase taxation to fund the scheme [½]
Either general taxation; for example, increasing the rate of tax on goods and services or through a similar mechanism [½]
Or an income related tax such that payment relates to income level [½]
or through a tax on corporations [½]
The government could borrow / issue debt to finance the health system, in the form of long-term government bonds. [½]
The government could redirect its existing tax revenue to fund the health system [½]
Although this will require reducing funds for other projects which may not be viable. [½]
The government could look to make savings in the running of the scheme e,g, through efficiencies or other gains through centralising purchasing [½]
or by regulating the cost of services. [½]
Specific employer/employee contributions deducted via payroll [½]
This could channel spend on health insurance premiums to the social insurance scheme. [½]
The scheme may rely on voluntary contributions if the scheme and benefit provision is not compulsory or tiered care is provided. [½]
The scheme may rely on out-of-pocket expenditure for a range of services currently provided by insurance . [½]
If so, customers may continue to pay for treatment upfront and increase the funding available to the extent that upfront expenditure supports current public services. [½]

(iii).question:
The government is considering two revenue collection mechanisms for the proposed fund. The first is a mandatory payroll deduction and the second is an increase in the rate of tax applied to goods and services (sales tax).
Compare the advantages and disadvantages of these two options.
(iii).synopsis: Payroll Deductions, Administrative Efficiency, Revenue Predictability

Solution: Payroll Deductions Advantages: If a percentage of salary, contributions will be related to income [½]
Which may be perceived as fair [½]
May be easy to implement and administer if this can be managed together with other payroll taxes [½]
If current health insurance is typically funded via payroll deductions, then employees may not be opposed to the contribution [½]
Cost of collecting the individual’s payments is passed on to the employer [½]
Which reduces costs for the government [½]
And makes it easier for the government to collect the money. [½]
This would be a predictable source of income for the government. [½]
Disadvantages: Payroll deductions unlikely to work for the self-employed or those running private businesses [½]
A work around for the self-employed could be expensive [½]
If a work around could not be found this would be a lost opportunity in terms of the revenue base for this tax. [½]
Success may depend on employment levels [½]
If medical inflation rises faster than wage inflation, this may mean that deductions need to increase as a percentage of salary over time [½]
May be unpopular, especially if the tax burden on the employed population is already high [½]
Sales tax Advantages: The broad based tax may be considered fair as everyone will contribute [½]
May be easy to implement and can be managed together with any existing Sales Tax collection processes [½]
Disadvantages There may be a relatively higher burden on the poor which may be undesirable [½]
Most retailers are likely to pass this tax on to customers in the form of higher prices and this may create economic issues as higher prices may depress demand [½]
Additional taxes may be unpopular [½]
Customers may substitute goods so that spending and tax collection is less than anticipated [½]
If there is a lack of trust in the provision of services (given the low quality of existing services), citizens may prefer a direct, transparent payment to the scheme [½]

Q3.label: Question 3
Q3.scenario:
Company Y is a health and care insurance company that has recently entered into a distribution agreement with a large grocery chain. The grocery chain has stores located throughout Country C.
The distribution agreement has the following key principles:
 Company Y will sell its insurance products directly to consumers through the grocery chain’s stores.
 Products sold via the partnership will carry the branding of both the grocery chain and Company Y.
The marketing director of Company Y has proposed that the company offers a free insurance product to the grocery chain’s customers as a way of creating awareness that Company Y will be selling its insurance products through the grocery chain’s stores. The proposed free product will be a health cash product valid for a 6 month term and will be offered to customers who spend a minimum amount of money at the grocery store. The product will be offered to the grocery chain’s customers for a limited promotional period.

(i).question: Discuss the factors that need to be considered by Company Y when designing the new free hospital cash product.
(i).synopsis: Product Development, Consumer Needs, Grocery Chain Partnership

Solution: Product Benefits What customer need is this product meeting? [½]
What benefits will be offered? [½]
For example (max 2 marks): Will the plan cover hospital stays and/or other medical needs? [½]
What limits are there on the benefits? [½]
Any exclusions? [½]
Any waiting period or deferred period? [½]
Can the benefit fit with services available in the grocery store? [½]
For example, if the grocery store has a pharmacy division, the benefits could be structured to fit with the services offered [½]
Will a Hospital cash plan variant be available as a paid-for product? [½]
Does the product fit in with the products planned for distribution via the partnership? [½]
Hospital cash plans typically provide a low value to customers but will need to consider any market norms or customer expectations. [½]
Capital requirements The company will need to consider the new business strain from the product. [½]
Depending on the capital levels the company has it may need to consider whether it should apply limits on the volumes of this product that are written to avoid solvency issues. [½]
These limits could be difficult to manage in practice. [½]
Expenses The product has no premium, therefore need to consider: [½]
How will the expenses associated with launch and claims payments be funded? [½]
Will this be funded via the marketing budget? [½]
Or will the grocery store also contribute to funding? [½]
There may be large system development costs to enable the product to be launched [½]
May need to consider a business case for development spend. [½]
If a business case is required, how will benefits be measured? If benefits are largely related to brand building, these may be difficult to measure. [½]
If the company usually reinsures Hospital Cash plan business, then reinsurance premiums may be an additional expense. [½]
Need to allow for the costs of training staff. [½]
Policy administration costs will be dependent on the final product offering. [½]
Distribution If the product will be distributed via stores then this will limit distribution costs. [½]
What will the process be; will it be automatic or will customers be required to opt- in? [½]
How will customers consent to data sharing with the insurer? [½]
If enrolment is automatic, will customers know they have the benefit? [½]
If it is embedded and customers are unaware of the free benefit, then this will not [½]
meet the marketing campaign objectives. Underwriting The structure / distribution is likely to mean that there will be no underwriting. [½]
And if the product has a 6 month validity it may not be fair to customers to put waiting periods in place. [½]
This could mean there is a risk of poor selection. [½]
Benefits could be accidental to take this into account. [½]
Upselling process After the campaign the insurer will have a pool of customers that it knows are interested in insurance [½]
and to whom the insurer may be able to sell additional products. [½]
Regulatory requirements What regulations need to be considered [½]
for example, automatic opt-in may not be allowed. [½]
How will treating customers fairly issues for the product be considered? [½]
How will the benefits be communicated to customers? [½]
What training and/or certification will store agents need? [½]
There is a risk of mis-selling. [½]
Will regulator approval be required? [½]
If so, will the approval timelines impact the marketing campaign? [½]
Target Market The minimum amount of money that customers must spend should be set at a level that is appropriate for the insurer’s target market [½]
Campaign requirements The campaign will run for a limited time but there may be a need to limit on customer numbers as well (to limit expected claims). [½]
Limiting volumes may not meet the marketing team’s requirements [½]
But may be needed from a capital strain perspective. [½]
Has the insurer investigated the number of customers who will meet the minimum spend requirement? [½]
This will impact campaign reach. If the qualifying spend is low, the insurer may be able to rapidly sign up many customers. [½]

(ii).question: Suggest possible concerns the insurance regulator may have with the launch of the proposed free product.
(ii).synopsis: Regulatory Concerns, Financial Soundness, Consumer Protection

Solution: Promoting soundness The regulator will be concerned whether launching the product could adversely impact the financial soundness of the insurer. [½]
For example, the regulator may be concerned about the ability of the company to meet policyholder liabilities given that no premiums will be received. [½]
Will the insurer set up appropriate reserves for the new product? [½]
The regulator could be concerned about the impact of the product on the insurer’s solvency position. [½]
Promoting customer protection The regulator will be concerned about whether the product is ensuring appropriate [½]
consumer outcomes. The regulator may be concerned about mis-selling. [½]
For example, the product being marketed as free but requiring a minimum spend [½]

And the minimum spend may mean that customers with a genuine need for the product, will not have access to it. There may be concern on the enrolment process and whether customers will be aware of and understand the benefits provided. [½]
There may be concern about the limited term and whether value is provided to the customer. [½]
And the regulator may be concerned that the expected claims ratio will be low. [½]
Depending on design, hospital definitions and waiting periods may also cause concern [½]
and could potentially give the public a bad impression of hospital cash plans. [½]
The regulator may want to understand how the limited promotional period will be explained to customers [½]
and could be concerned that the consumers may not fully understand the limitations of the product [½]
such as how the limited product term will be explained to customers. [½]
The regulator could be concerned that the cost of the ‘free’ product could be passed on to other policyholders, which could be regarded as unfair. [½]
The insurer is getting access to policyholders spending patterns. There is a risk that this information is used for purposes that the policyholder did not agree to. [½]
Other The regulator could be concerned that the insurer will not adequately service the product as it is ‘free’. [½]

(iii).question: Suggest product or campaign design features that can be used to reduce the insurance regulator’s concerns.
(iii).synopsis: Risk Mitigation, Marketing Promotions, Profitability Monitoring

Solution: Financial soundness The company could reduce the benefits offered. [½]
The campaign should be closely monitored to ensure that it does not cause strain. [½]
The company could run the promotion until a specific number of policies have been taken out. [½]
The company could retain the right to terminate the promotion early depending on take up [½]
Or terminate depending on the claims experience emerging. [½]
Alternatively, the company could negotiate with the grocery chain to assess whether a premium could be funded by the grocery chain given the minimum spend requirement [½]
Or allow customers to top up to a more generous plan by purchasing additional cover. [½]
The insurer could pilot the campaign first (for example, in a limited number of stores or in a specific region). [½]
The insurer could ensure it holds adequate reserves to meet future liabilities. [½]
Customer outcomes The company could remove the minimum spend requirement. [½]
The company could position its own staff at the grocery chain in order to explain the product to customers [½]
and ensure the company’s own staff are appropriately trained about the features of the new product. [½]
The company could ensure policy literature is clear about the limitations of the deal [½]
Or have FAQs / Terms and conditions available via flyers or digital format within stores. [½]
The company could amend the benefit to accident only in order to remove complexity around waiting periods or other product features which may include mis-selling risk [½]

(iv).question:
Company Y has also been considering an advertising campaign as an alternative way of increasing awareness of the distribution agreement with the grocery chain and to increase new business volumes from this agreement. The advertising campaign would include advertising via television, radio and social media. In addition, in-store marketing via flyers and posters will also be used.
Discuss the relative advantages or disadvantages for Company Y of an advertising campaign compared to offering a free product to customers of the grocery chain.
(iv).synopsis: Advertising Strategy, Social Media Reach, System Development Costs

Solution: Advantages An advertising campaign may have greater reach, particularly if social media is employed [½]
And specifically reach beyond the grocery chain’s customer base [½]
So may drive greater awareness and new business compared to the product offering. [½]
The campaign spend will be certain [½]
Relative to uncertain claim costs if the free product is offered. [½]
There may be substantial product and system development costs incurred in setting up the free product, making the free product a more expensive option. [½]
This may also increase the time to market compared to that required to launch the advertising campaign. [½]
The advertising campaign can be used to tailor customer messaging and so will limit risk compared to potential mis-selling/mis-information by grocery chain staff. [½]
The advertising campaign could be tailored more accurately for the insurers ideal target market. [½]
The advertising campaign can cover a range of products, not just health cash plans. [½]
The profitability of the products sold due to the media campaign may be higher than the ‘free’ product. [½]
Disadvantages Other competitors may copy the campaign / it may be difficult for the campaign to stand out in a competitive market place. [½]
It may be difficult to track new business derived from an advertising campaign [½]
Whereas new business from the free product could create a lead pool for upselling of paid for products. [½]
And the minimum spend criteria may act as a proxy for income/future premium collection rates [½]
and conversion rates / success of the campaign can likely be better tracked. [½]
The ‘free‘ product will offer consumers the chance to experience the product. [½]
It may be possible to generate testimonials from customers, particularly where claims are paid. [½]
So may increase trust and willingness to purchase paid products in future. [½]
If the grocery chain has a captive customer base, the free product may allow for the [½]
best leverage of synergies between the insurer (product) and grocery chain (customer base and distribution). It may be difficult to measure how successful the advertising campaign has been. [½]
